That my North Star is to produce a business, and I'm going to pick, you know, just make a random number. Is that in this year, my North Star is to create a business that's going to generate a half a million dollars in net profit. Maybe next year that North Star is going to produce three quarters of a million in net profit. And you keep driving it that way because then your thinking is not only about driving the revenue growth, but managing the cost and the business that way, right?

Yeah and you know when you first started talking my head kind of went to profit, probably because I've done scalable and had the 12-quarter plan and different things there like so that made sense when you started doing it and I. And I don't know why. Well, I mean, we can, this is a great conversation with that in this, this what's called the most, it's called the mind methodology. It's the most important number and then the drivers for it. But it's, it's this notion of. As an organization, what am I trying to get all? I don't know if Ryan still talks about it. Elon Musk's idea of vectors, which is getting everyone aligned, heading in the same direction, trying to drive the same kind of outcome. And so it's like, okay, if we're driving towards a profit— most important number. And then, you know, we can break that down to, okay, my sales organization needs to be delivering a sales number and their sales number supports the profit number.
My operations people, you know, and you find that you can then structure everyone to have a most important metric that supports that. And not that there aren't other KPIs and other things that kind of indicate that, but then they are always acting in a way towards. Driving to the thing that's going to support that. So, if you started with your marketing team, well, the marketing team's job is to deliver the qualified leads. Ultimately, that's what they do. Now, there might be other metrics that they have in terms of conversion rates or cost per click or whatever you're going to measure. But their job in the end is to deliver leads. Sales' job is to close leads. Operations is to run the facilities cost-effectively. And you break it down, it becomes very simple to say.
Here's the value that you deliver to the organization. And yeah, you have some internal things that help you understand and dial that we can turn to. To better deliver on that, but your value as a marketing team. is deliver leads. You either are or you're not. sales. Your job is to deliver sales. You either are or you're not. And it makes that conversation become much simpler if you've got an organization that's kind of built along those lines, Danny. Makes a lot of sense. A lot of sense. So I would tend to net that out at the sales line. Because that's really what's coming into the business. Good to keep track of it. Good to know. So they are charged against operations as, you know, that cost of goods sold or whatever is it. You know, a good ratio for warranty expenses is something less than 1%. That's typically, at the time I ran that manufacturing firm, that's, you know, we were working to keep our warranty costs below one.
But it was considered an expense. It did not get netted against sales. I can't, so I see sales. Which would be... you know, and of course, if it's online marketing and sales, but typically what you're trying to do is say, well, marketing needs to be delivering about a thousand leads per week.
I'm just picking a number, right? So, or I need... 150 leads per day. To keep it in your daily, I can, I'm going to make this much. I'm going to make $1,200 in sales per day. I need to be generating 150 leads per day. because now I'm going to lose through conversion. And so if you can follow the funnel through. and keep it focused on marketing's job is to deliver leads. deliver people into the top of the funnel. principally. And then it's the rest of the funnel or it's the rest of the activity. to convert that lead. to a customer. Then I would know that, okay, marketing, the ads we're delivering. But maybe our our landing page is not converting.
and now you've got funnels down below so again is you know i'm going to get that number there or not. But I think keeping it clearer. in terms of did marketing do its job? Delivering leads. Did the leads convert or not? And if they're not converting, I kind of have two questions I ask. right what is Is that sales copy converting? Or is the sales page converting? So it could be, maybe, the offers, you know, a couple of things about that, or. Are the leads being delivered not truly qualified, not the right avatar? Sou só. a foolish example but just emphasize it is hey my marketing is delivering dog lovers to a funnel that's going to sell a horse course, right? Well, of course they're not.
I mean, I'll probably get a few people that are both dog and horse lovers and et cetera. Might get a few though, but principally I'm delivering someone to the funnel that isn't. going to convert so You can go back up and you can kind of confirm and do some things there. So you really want to make sure that you've got that clarity. So I would, I would say that. It's, it's. You've got a little bit too much here, Kelly. So I think keeping it to those handful of critical numbers. Now, if you want to put supporting KPIs beside it, so if I want to look in the marketing funnel or I want to look here and I want to see a little bit more granularity about some of the numbers.
Then that would be fine. But at the company, you know, I'd be asking. Is marketing doing its job? Is marketing delivering the leads? Is sales, in this case, the sales funnel, is it converting or not converting? And then that helps. You but helps, you know, your growing leadership team. To focus their thinking. On kind of the right kind of questions. Yeah. Now with marketing and sales in a more standard. OK, leads come in and now sales calls or sales meetings are getting made. That distinction to me always made a lot of sense to me in the kind of funnels where someone's coming into an opt-in page and then they're getting or coming into a sales page and there's no actual contact with a human. Then, to me, that line felt more blurry.
Well, and it can, and honestly, I mean, we struggle with it as too, but let me try to help give a little bit more clarity. So. We can get caught because we get metrics, which is— I spent this many dollars here and I got this many dollars in sales, right? The return on ad spend, right? Boom, I can see that. I can actually get some benchmarks about it. And it feels like— like one big number, right? Which actually does almost nothing for doing any diagnostic when things aren't working, right? So what you really want to— ask yourself and we kind of do that in in the growth funnel to a certain level, but even more granular, it is, what is that next thing I'm selling? So if if it's a if it's a.
If it's an ad in social media, the thing I'm actually trying to sell them is the action to click to the landing page, right? If the ad has done that, and gotten, you know, the right person to the landing page. The ad has really done its job. The ad is not to sell. The ad is to get someone to the landing page. Okay. So now I can say, 'Okay. The right person, getting enough of them.' Okay, great. Now, if I'm here with, again, pick a number, 100 people sitting at the landing page. And um, because we're actually going through this with a client of ours. They're getting a bunch of people off of off of Google Ads. To this landing page, it's another actually another person scale we're working with.
Uh, but Their page isn't converting. Well, that's not likely a fault of the ad, assuming that the person getting there is right. That suggests that the landing page isn't encouraging the person to take that next action, right? So now you look in there. So the job of a funnel is for the step above to sell the next step below. Not the end. It's like: get there, then that, then the next step is that, you know, essentially, the action is the next step. And if you can keep it focused on thinking about all I want to do is... get them to take the next step. Then you can go back and figure out where. where the breakdown might be in them not taking that step, Kelly. Okay. Okay, yeah, that helps a lot.
Again, it's the simplicity. Well, I mean, we're actually encouraged. Like I said, we're encouraged to think about it as one big sort of gnarly thing, right?
